app.name: CyberAI
app.certificatePresenter: Presented by the CyberAI Security Team
app.certificateIdFormat: CERT-{8 random uppercase alphanumeric chars}, e.g. CERT-DEIDY1AM

levels.title: Cyber Sentinel
levels.source: wireframe (6/8 modules, 2850 pts -> Lv.5; stays Lv.5 at 8/8)

formulas.questionScoring: correct answer = full question points; wrong = 0. Confidence selection does NOT affect points (design decision: wireframes show no point modifier; confidence feeds the AI Quiz Coach instead).
formulas.moduleCompletion: module is Completed after the user finishes its quiz once; score persists; Review Quiz replays answers read-only; lesson shows Lesson Completed state

modules.title: Phishing: The Lure (Email)

sections.heading: 1. What is Phishing?
sections.body: Phishing is a fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, and credit card details by disguising as a trustworthy entity in an electronic communication, typically email. It's the most common form of cyber attack.

sections.heading: 2. Red Flags of a Phishing Email

bullets.title: Urgent or Threatening Language
bullets.text: Phrases like 'Account Suspended' or 'Unauthorized Login Attempt' create panic, rushing you into clicking without thinking.

bullets.title: Generic Greetings
bullets.text: Vague greetings like 'Dear Customer' instead of your actual name can be a major red flag.

bullets.title: Suspicious Links & Attachments
bullets.text: Hover over links to see the real URL. Never open unexpected attachments, as they can contain malware.

bullets.title: Sender Email Mismatch
bullets.text: The sender's name might say 'PayPal', but the email address is something like 'secure-update@paypalservice123.com'. Always check the full email address.

bullets.title: Spelling and Grammar Mistakes
bullets.text: Obvious errors are a sign of a scam.

sections.heading: 3. Your Best Defense: Skepticism
sections.body: The golden rule is: if an email seems suspicious, it probably is. Do not click any links or download attachments. If you think the email might be legitimate, navigate to the company's website directly in a new browser tab and log in from there. Report the phishing attempt to your IT department.

bullets.title: Spear Phishing
bullets.text: Unlike mass phishing, spear phishing is personalised — the attacker uses your name, job title, company, or recent activity to make the email convincing. This information is gathered from LinkedIn, social media, or data breaches.

bullets.title: Whaling
bullets.text: Whaling targets high-value individuals — CEOs, CFOs, board members. The stakes are higher because these targets can authorise large wire transfers or access critical systems.

bullets.title: Clone Phishing
bullets.text: The attacker duplicates a legitimate email you previously received and replaces the link or attachment with a malicious one. The sender address appears identical to the original.

bullets.title: Quishing (QR Code Phishing)
bullets.text: Phishing via QR codes embedded in emails or printed materials. Because QR codes hide the destination URL, many email scanners don't detect them as malicious.

bullets.title: Pharming
bullets.text: DNS poisoning that silently redirects you to a fake website even when you type the correct URL. Your browser shows the right address but you're on an attacker's server.

bullets.title: Business Email Compromise (BEC)
bullets.text: The attacker impersonates an executive or supplier to request urgent wire transfers or changes to payment details. BEC costs organisations billions annually — always verify payment requests through a separate, trusted channel.

bullets.title: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC
bullets.text: These are email authentication standards. SPF specifies which servers can send email for your domain. DKIM adds a cryptographic signature. DMARC tells receivers what to do when SPF or DKIM fails (quarantine or reject). A DMARC failure warning in your email client is a strong sign of spoofing.

bullets.title: AiTM (Adversary-in-the-Middle) Phishing
bullets.text: A proxy sits between you and a legitimate login page, capturing not just your password but your session cookie after you successfully authenticate — bypassing 2FA. The only reliable defence is phishing-resistant hardware keys (FIDO2).

bullets.title: Consent Phishing / OAuth Phishing
bullets.text: Instead of stealing your password, the attacker tricks you into granting a malicious app permissions to your Google, Microsoft, or other OAuth account. Once granted, they control your account without needing your credentials.

bullets.title: Tracking Pixels
bullets.text: Tiny invisible images in emails that notify the sender when you open the message — revealing your IP address, location, device, and that your email is active. Never load remote images from unknown senders.

quiz.question: After identifying a suspicious email as a phishing attempt, what is the recommended next step?
options: Forward it to all your colleagues to warn them.
Mark it as spam and delete it.
Reply to the sender telling them you know it's a scam.
Report it to your IT/Security department using the official reporting channel.
quiz.explanation: Reporting phishing attempts to your IT/Security department is crucial. They can analyze the email, block the sender, and implement broader defenses to protect the entire organization. Simply marking as spam or deleting is insufficient.

quiz.question: What should you do if you accidentally click a link in a phishing email?
options: Immediately restart your computer.
Disconnect from the internet and report it to IT/Security right away.
Delete the email from your inbox and trash.
Run an antivirus scan and hope for the best.
quiz.explanation: Disconnecting from the network can prevent any potential malware from spreading or 'phoning home'. Reporting it immediately allows the security team to take action to protect you and the company.

quiz.question: Which of these is the BIGGEST red flag for a phishing email?
options: The email contains the company's official logo.
The sender's email address is from a public domain (like @gmail.com) but claims to be a bank.
The email is addressed to you by your first name.
The email offers you a discount on a future purchase.
quiz.explanation: Legitimate businesses, especially banks, will always email from their official corporate domain (e.g., @bankofamerica.com), not a free public one. This is a very strong indicator of a phishing attempt.

quiz.question: You receive an email from 'Netflix' with the subject 'Your account is on hold'. It asks you to click a link to update your payment details. What is the safest course of action?
options: Click the link and enter your details; you don't want to miss your shows.
Ignore the email and assume it's a mistake.
Open a new browser window, go to the official Netflix website, and check your account status there.
Reply to the email asking for confirmation.
quiz.explanation: Never trust links in unexpected emails regarding account security. The safest action is always to go directly to the official website through a trusted method to verify any claims.

cheatSheet: Be wary of emails creating a sense of urgency or fear.
Hover over links to inspect the true destination URL before clicking.
Verify the sender's email address; don't just trust the display name.
Never open unexpected attachments, especially from unknown senders.

modules.title: The Strong Vault: Passwords & 2FA

sections.heading: 1. Beyond 'password123'
sections.body: A strong password is your first and most crucial line of defense. Attackers use automated software to guess millions of passwords per second. Simple, common, or personal passwords can be cracked almost instantly.

sections.heading: 2. The Pillars of a Strong Password

bullets.title: Length is Strength
bullets.text: Aim for at least 14 characters. Each additional character makes a password exponentially harder to crack.

bullets.title: Complexity is Key
bullets.text: Use a mix of uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and symbols (e.g., !@#$%).

bullets.title: Uniqueness is Non-Negotiable
bullets.text: Never reuse passwords across different websites. A breach on one site would expose all your accounts.

sections.heading: 3. Your Secret Weapon: The Password Manager
sections.body: It's impossible to remember dozens of unique, complex passwords. A trusted password manager solves this. It generates, stores, and auto-fills ultra-strong passwords for you, so you only need to remember one master password.

sections.heading: 4. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA/MFA)
sections.body: 2FA is a security force multiplier. It requires a second form of verification (like a code from your phone) besides your password. This means even if an attacker steals your password, they cannot get into your account. Enable it everywhere.

bullets.title: Brute Force
bullets.text: Automated tools try every possible combination of characters. Short passwords (under 10 characters) can be cracked in seconds. Length is your best defence — longer passwords make brute force computationally impractical.

bullets.title: Dictionary Attacks
bullets.text: Attackers try lists of common words, phrases, and known passwords. 'Password1!' and 'Summer2024' are in every dictionary list. Avoid predictable patterns.

bullets.title: Credential Stuffing
bullets.text: When a website is breached, stolen username/password pairs are tested against other services. If you reuse passwords, one breach compromises all your accounts. A password manager solves this — unique passwords everywhere.

bullets.title: Password Spraying
bullets.text: Rather than attacking one account with many passwords (which triggers lockouts), attackers try one common password against thousands of accounts. Avoid passwords like 'Welcome1' or 'Company2024'.

bullets.title: Rainbow Table Attacks
bullets.text: Pre-computed tables of password hashes that allow instant lookup of unsalted passwords. This is why systems add a unique 'salt' to each password before hashing — your password hash won't match the table even if the password itself does.

bullets.title: MFA Fatigue / Push Bombing
bullets.text: Attackers who have your password send a flood of push notification approvals to your phone, hoping you'll tap 'Approve' just to stop them. Always check what you're approving — if you didn't initiate a login, deny it and change your password immediately.

bullets.title: Passkeys
bullets.text: Passkeys replace passwords entirely. A cryptographic key pair is generated — the private key stays on your device, the public key is on the website. You authenticate with your biometric or PIN locally. Passkeys are phishing-resistant because they only work on the exact legitimate site.

bullets.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
bullets.text: One master login (Google, Microsoft, Apple) grants access to many services. Convenient, but it means your SSO provider account is extremely high value — secure it with the strongest 2FA available (hardware key).

bullets.title: SIM Swapping
bullets.text: An attacker calls your mobile carrier, impersonates you, and convinces them to transfer your phone number to an attacker-controlled SIM. This defeats SMS-based 2FA entirely. Use authenticator apps or hardware keys instead of SMS 2FA for important accounts.

bullets.title: Backup / Recovery Codes
bullets.text: When you enable 2FA, services provide single-use recovery codes. Store them offline (printed or in a safe). Never photograph them or store them in cloud notes — these codes bypass 2FA entirely.

quiz.question: What is the most effective way to manage unique, complex passwords for all your accounts?
options: Write them down in a notebook.
Use a trusted password manager application.
Use the same password everywhere but change it monthly.
Use variations of your pet's name.
quiz.explanation: Password managers are designed to securely create, store, and manage unique and strong passwords for every account, eliminating password reuse and the need to remember them all.

quiz.question: Which password example follows best practices for strength and uniqueness?
options: YourCompany123
P@sswordForWork!
aPpLePi3&C0ff33!
QwertY@123
quiz.explanation: 'aPpLePi3&C0ff33!' is a strong passphrase that is long, complex, and avoids common patterns, making it highly resistant to cracking. The others use common patterns or are too short/simple.

quiz.question: Which of the following is the WEAKEST password?
options: Fg#9k$2(j@1Po!
Summer2024!
MyDogFido123
Tr0ub4dor&3
quiz.explanation: 'Summer2024!' is predictable and follows a common pattern (word + year + symbol), making it much easier to guess with automated tools than a truly random string of characters.

quiz.question: What is the primary benefit of Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)?
options: It makes your password stronger.
It prevents your password from being stolen.
It adds a second layer of security, making your account inaccessible even if your password is stolen.
It automatically logs you into your accounts.
quiz.explanation: 2FA's main strength is that it requires a second factor (something you have, like your phone) in addition to your password (something you know), stopping attackers who have only stolen the password.

cheatSheet: Use a password manager to create unique passwords for every service.
Enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) on all critical accounts.
Make passwords long (14+ characters) and complex (mix of cases, numbers, symbols).
Never reuse passwords. A breach on one site will compromise others.

modules.title: Malware & Ransomware Defense

sections.heading: 1. Understanding Malicious Software
sections.body: Malware is any software designed to harm your system. It includes viruses, spyware, and trojans. Ransomware is a particularly nasty type that encrypts all your files, holding them hostage until you pay a fee.

bullets.title: Phishing Emails
bullets.text: Clicking malicious links or opening infected attachments is the #1 way malware spreads.

bullets.title: Malicious Downloads
bullets.text: Software from untrusted websites, often disguised as 'free' games or utilities, can be bundled with malware.

bullets.title: Unpatched Software
bullets.text: Attackers exploit security holes in outdated software (your OS, browser, or applications). Keeping everything updated is critical.

sections.heading: 3. Your Defensive Strategy
sections.body: A multi-layered defense is key. Use a reputable antivirus/anti-malware program and keep it updated. Be extremely cautious about what you click and download. Regularly back up your important files to an external drive or cloud service; this is your best defense against ransomware.

sections.heading: 4. More Malware Types You Should Know

bullets.title: Rootkits
bullets.text: Malware that hides deep within the operating system, often below the level that antivirus can detect. Rootkits give attackers persistent, privileged access and can survive reboots and even OS reinstalls.

bullets.title: Keyloggers
bullets.text: Record every keystroke you make — capturing passwords, credit card numbers, and private messages. They can be software-based (installed via phishing) or hardware-based (physical device plugged between keyboard and computer).

bullets.title: Botnets
bullets.text: Your infected device becomes part of a remotely controlled network (botnet) used to send spam, conduct DDoS attacks, or mine cryptocurrency — all without your knowledge. Your machine works for the attacker while you pay the electricity bill.

bullets.title: Cryptojacking
bullets.text: Malware silently uses your CPU to mine cryptocurrency for the attacker. Signs: unusually high CPU usage, slow performance, hot device, high electricity bills. Can arrive via malicious browser scripts or installed malware.

bullets.title: Fileless Malware
bullets.text: Operates entirely in memory — no file is written to disk, making it invisible to traditional file-scanning antivirus. It often uses legitimate system tools (like PowerShell) to carry out attacks. Requires behaviour-based EDR to detect.

bullets.title: Logic Bombs
bullets.text: Dormant code hidden inside legitimate software that activates when a specific condition is met — a date, a user action, or a file deletion. Often planted by malicious insiders.

bullets.title: Zero-Day Vulnerabilities
bullets.text: A flaw in software that is unknown to the vendor — no patch exists. Attackers exploit zero-days before defenders can respond. Keeping software updated closes known vulnerabilities; security software with behaviour detection catches unknown ones.

bullets.title: Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS)
bullets.text: Criminal groups sell ransomware toolkits to non-technical attackers as a franchise model. This has dramatically lowered the barrier to launching ransomware attacks and explains why they've surged.

bullets.title: Double Extortion
bullets.text: Modern ransomware doesn't just encrypt your files — it also steals them first. Attackers threaten to publish sensitive data publicly if you don't pay, even if you restore from backup.

bullets.title: Supply Chain Attacks
bullets.text: Malware injected into a legitimate software update or third-party library that you trust. The famous SolarWinds attack compromised thousands of organisations this way. Vet your software suppliers and monitor for unexpected behaviour after updates.

bullets.title: Lateral Movement
bullets.text: After infecting one machine, attackers move across your network to reach more valuable systems. Segmenting networks, using least-privilege accounts, and monitoring for unusual internal connections limit how far they can spread.

bullets.title: Indicators of Compromise (IoC)
bullets.text: Signs that a system may be infected: unexpected CPU/network activity, new unknown processes, disabled security tools, unusual outbound connections, or files appearing/disappearing. Report any of these to IT immediately.

quiz.question: What is the defining characteristic of a ransomware attack?
options: It steals your password.
It slows down your computer.
It encrypts your files and demands a payment for their release.
It displays unwanted pop-up ads.
quiz.explanation: Ransomware's goal is extortion. It makes your files unusable by encrypting them and then demands a ransom payment, usually in cryptocurrency, for the decryption key.

quiz.question: Why is it crucial to keep your operating system and applications updated?
options: To get new features and a better user interface.
To fix security vulnerabilities that malware can exploit.
To ensure compatibility with new websites.
To make your software run faster.
quiz.explanation: Software updates frequently include 'patches' for security holes that have been discovered. Attackers specifically target these known vulnerabilities in outdated software to deliver malware.

quiz.question: What is the single most effective way to recover from a ransomware attack without paying the ransom?
options: Unplugging your computer from the internet.
Running an antivirus scan after the attack.
Having recent, secure backups of your important files.
Negotiating with the attackers for a lower price.
quiz.explanation: If you have clean backups, you can restore your files without needing to pay the attackers. This neutralizes their primary leverage and is a critical part of any security strategy.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT a common way malware can infect a computer?
options: Through official software updates from trusted vendors.
By clicking malicious links in phishing emails.
Downloading cracked software from untrusted websites.
Exploiting security vulnerabilities in outdated software.
quiz.explanation: Official software updates from trusted vendors are designed to fix security flaws and add features, not introduce malware. Malware typically spreads through malicious downloads, phishing, or exploiting vulnerabilities.

cheatSheet: Ransomware encrypts your files and demands payment; backups are your best defense.
Keep your operating system, browser, and all applications fully updated.
Use a reputable antivirus program and keep its definitions current.
Be highly suspicious of unsolicited email attachments and downloads from untrusted sites.

modules.title: Vishing & Smishing (Non-Email Threats)

sections.heading: 1. Phishing Beyond Email
sections.body: Social engineering doesn't just happen in your inbox. Attackers also use phone calls and text messages to trick you. This is known as Vishing (Voice Phishing) and Smishing (SMS Phishing).

bullets.title: Urgent Bank Alerts (Smishing)
bullets.text: A text message claims your account is frozen and provides a link to 'verify' your identity. The link leads to a fake login page.

bullets.title: Tech Support Scams (Vishing)
bullets.text: An unsolicited call from someone claiming to be from Microsoft or Apple, stating your computer is infected. They aim to get remote access or payment.

bullets.title: Impersonation
bullets.text: Attackers may pretend to be from the IRS, a delivery service, or even your own company's IT helpdesk.

sections.heading: 3. How to Respond
sections.body: The golden rule is: never trust, always verify. Do not click links in suspicious texts. Hang up on unsolicited calls. If the message claims to be from a company you do business with, contact them directly using a phone number or website you know is legitimate. Never use the contact info provided in the message or call.

bullets.title: Voice Cloning & Deepfake Audio
bullets.text: AI can now clone someone's voice from as little as a few seconds of audio. Attackers impersonate your manager, a family member, or a bank representative in real time. If a caller makes an urgent financial or access request, hang up and call the person back on a number you already know.

bullets.title: OTP Bots
bullets.text: Automated systems call you pretending to be your bank, and use a robotic voice to ask you to 'confirm' a one-time passcode that was just sent to your phone. The bot immediately relays that code to the attacker logging into your account. No legitimate organisation will ever ask you to read back a code you just received.

bullets.title: Hybrid Smishing-to-Vishing
bullets.text: A text message creates alarm ('Your account is compromised, call this number immediately'), then the follow-up call does the actual social engineering. The two-step process feels more convincing because you initiated the call.

bullets.title: Pig Butchering Scams
bullets.text: Attackers build a relationship with you over weeks or months via text or messaging apps, posing as a romantic interest or new friend. Eventually they introduce a 'can't-miss' crypto investment opportunity. Once you invest, they vanish with your funds. The long trust-building phase is what makes this devastatingly effective.

bullets.title: Wangiri Fraud
bullets.text: A missed call from an unfamiliar international number. Calling back connects you to a premium-rate line, racking up large charges per minute. Never return missed calls from numbers you don't recognise, especially international ones.

bullets.title: Reverse Vishing
bullets.text: Instead of the attacker calling you, they send a fake alert (email or SMS) with a 'security hotline' number to call. You call them, believing you initiated a safe contact — but you're calling the attacker. Always find contact numbers from official websites, not from messages.

bullets.title: QR Code Smishing
bullets.text: A text message contains a QR code image rather than a text link. Because the URL is hidden inside the image, many SMS spam filters miss it. Treat QR codes in unsolicited messages with the same scepticism as links.

bullets.title: SIM Swapping
bullets.text: An attacker calls your mobile carrier with enough personal information (gathered from social media or data breaches) to convince them to transfer your phone number to a new SIM. They then receive all your SMS messages, including 2FA codes, and can reset your passwords. Use app-based or hardware 2FA, not SMS, for critical accounts.

quiz.question: You receive a text message from an unknown number that says: 'Your bank account has been locked due to suspicious activity. Click here to unlock it: [link]'. What should you do?
options: Click the link immediately to secure your account.
Reply 'STOP' to the text message.
Delete the message and contact your bank using their official website or app.
Call the phone number the text came from.
quiz.explanation: This is a classic smishing tactic. The link leads to a fake site designed to steal your login credentials. Always verify such claims by contacting the institution through an official, trusted channel you find yourself.

quiz.question: A person calls you claiming to be from 'Microsoft Tech Support' and says they've detected a virus on your computer. They ask to remotely access your machine. You should:
options: Follow their instructions, as Microsoft is a trusted company.
Ask for their employee ID number to verify them.
Politely hang up the phone.
Give them your IP address so they can run a scan.
quiz.explanation: Legitimate tech support from companies like Microsoft or Apple will never call you unsolicited. This is a scam to gain control of your computer, steal data, or install malware. The only safe action is to hang up.

quiz.question: What is the key difference between Phishing, Vishing, and Smishing?
options: The type of information they try to steal.
The country they originate from.
The medium they use: email, voice call, or SMS text message.
The level of danger they pose.
quiz.explanation: They are all forms of social engineering designed to trick you. The only difference is the communication channel used: Phishing (email), Vishing (voice), and Smishing (SMS).

cheatSheet: Vishing = Voice Phishing (scams via phone calls).
Smishing = SMS Phishing (scams via text messages).
Never click links or call numbers from unsolicited texts or calls.
Verify any urgent request by contacting the company through their official website or phone number.

modules.title: Physical, Travel, & Remote Security

sections.heading: 1. Security is Not Just Digital
sections.body: Protecting information goes beyond firewalls. Physical security means protecting your devices and your screen from unauthorized access in the real world.

sections.heading: 2. In the Office and On the Go

bullets.title: Lock Your Screen
bullets.text: Always lock your computer when you step away, even for a moment. (Win+L on Windows, Cmd+Ctrl+Q on Mac).

bullets.title: Beware of Shoulder Surfing
bullets.text: Be aware of your surroundings in public places like cafes or airports. People can watch you enter passwords or view sensitive data over your shoulder.

bullets.title: Secure Your Devices
bullets.text: Never leave laptops, phones, or tablets unattended in public. When traveling, keep them in your possession, not in checked luggage.

sections.heading: 3. The Dangers of Public Wi-Fi
sections.body: Public Wi-Fi is not secure. Attackers on the same network can potentially intercept your data. Avoid logging into sensitive accounts (bank, email) on public Wi-Fi. If you must use it, always use a trusted Virtual Private Network (VPN) to encrypt your connection.

bullets.title: Full-Disk Encryption
bullets.text: If a laptop is stolen, full-disk encryption (BitLocker on Windows, FileVault on Mac) makes the data completely unreadable without the correct password. Enable it on all work devices — without it, anyone can read your files by booting from USB.

bullets.title: RFID Badge Cloning
bullets.text: Proximity access card readers can be skimmed from up to 30cm away using a hidden device. Anti-passback systems prevent a cloned card from being used to enter an area if it was also used to exit — providing a layer of protection against cloned badges.

bullets.title: Mantrap / Vestibule
bullets.text: A secure two-door airlock entry where only one door can open at a time. Used in high-security areas to prevent tailgating — you must be positively identified before the inner door opens.

bullets.title: Visitor Management
bullets.text: All visitors should sign in with ID, receive a temporary visitor badge, and be escorted at all times in secure areas. Never let someone follow you through a secure door just because they seem friendly or official.

bullets.title: Dumpster Diving
bullets.text: Attackers search physical waste bins and skips for printed documents containing passwords, org charts, customer data, or financial information. Always shred sensitive documents before disposal — never put them in recycling or general waste.

bullets.title: Printer Output Tray Risk
bullets.text: Sensitive documents are frequently forgotten in printer output trays, visible to anyone walking past. Collect your prints immediately, and use follow-you / pull printing where you authenticate at the printer before the job is released.

bullets.title: Secure Media Destruction
bullets.text: Deleting files or even formatting a drive does not remove the data — it just removes the directory pointer. Secure disposal requires overwriting (software tools that write random data multiple times), degaussing (powerful magnets for magnetic drives), or physical destruction (shredding). Your IT team should handle this.

bullets.title: IT Asset Disposal Policy
bullets.text: All devices must be formally decommissioned and wiped before they leave the organisation — including donating, selling, or disposing of old hardware. A device sold without proper wiping can expose years of sensitive data to the buyer.

bullets.title: OSINT from Discarded Materials
bullets.text: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) techniques can reconstruct org charts, internal processes, and project names from shredded documents, discarded whiteboards, and even sticky notes. Physical information hygiene is as important as digital.

quiz.question: What is 'shoulder surfing'?
options: A new web browsing technique.
Using a screen protector to block side views.
The act of observing someone's screen or keyboard to steal information.
A type of malware that watches your screen.
quiz.explanation: Shoulder surfing is a low-tech but effective method of stealing confidential information, like passwords or PINs, by simply looking over someone's shoulder.

quiz.question: When working in a public coffee shop, what is the best way to protect your data on their Wi-Fi network?
options: Only visit websites that use HTTPS.
Use a Virtual Private Network (VPN).
Ask the barista if the Wi-Fi is secure.
Connect only for a few minutes at a time.
quiz.explanation: While HTTPS is good, a VPN is the most comprehensive solution. It encrypts ALL your internet traffic, creating a secure tunnel that prevents anyone on the same public network from snooping on your activity.

quiz.question: What is the keyboard shortcut to quickly lock your computer screen on Windows?
options: Ctrl + Alt + Delete
Alt + F4
Windows Key + L
Ctrl + L
quiz.explanation: Windows Key + L is the fastest way to lock your screen, a critical habit to build whenever you step away from your desk to prevent unauthorized physical access.

cheatSheet: Always lock your computer when you step away (Win+L or Cmd+Ctrl+Q).
Use a VPN to encrypt your connection on public Wi-Fi networks.
Be aware of 'shoulder surfers' watching your screen in public places.
Never leave devices like laptops or phones unattended.

modules.title: Data Handling, Reporting, & Compliance

sections.heading: 1. Not All Data is Equal
sections.body: Data is often classified by its sensitivity: Public (press releases), Internal (org charts), and Confidential/Restricted (personally identifiable information (PII), financial records, health information). You have a responsibility to protect sensitive data.

bullets.title: Need to Know
bullets.text: Only access and share sensitive data with those who have a legitimate business need.

bullets.title: Secure Storage
bullets.text: Store confidential data on approved, encrypted systems, not on personal devices or unsecured cloud services.

bullets.title: Proper Disposal
bullets.text: Don't just throw sensitive information away. Physical documents should be shredded. Digital files should be securely deleted, not just moved to the trash bin.

sections.heading: 3. When Things Go Wrong: Incident Reporting
sections.body: If you suspect a security incident (e.g., you clicked a phishing link, lost a work device, or see suspicious activity), you must report it immediately to the designated contact (IT, InfoSec, your manager). Fast reporting is critical to minimizing damage.

bullets.title: The CIA Triad
bullets.text: The foundation of information security: Confidentiality (only authorised people can access data), Integrity (data is accurate and unmodified), and Availability (data is accessible when needed). Every security control protects one or more of these three properties.

bullets.title: Right to Erasure (Right to be Forgotten)
bullets.text: Under GDPR, individuals can request that their personal data be deleted. Organisations must comply within one month unless there is a legal basis for retention. This applies to backups and archives too.

bullets.title: Data Subject Access Request (DSAR)
bullets.text: Any individual can request a copy of all personal data an organisation holds about them. Organisations must respond within one month and cannot charge a fee for the first request.

bullets.title: Data Protection Officer (DPO)
bullets.text: Organisations processing large volumes of personal data must appoint a DPO — an expert responsible for ensuring GDPR compliance. Know who your DPO is and contact them for privacy questions.

bullets.title: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
bullets.text: Required before starting any high-risk data processing activity (e.g., large-scale profiling, processing sensitive data). A DPIA identifies and mitigates privacy risks before they become breaches.

bullets.title: Controller vs Processor
bullets.text: The controller decides why and how personal data is processed. The processor handles data on the controller's behalf. Both have GDPR obligations, and their relationship must be governed by a Data Processing Agreement (DPA).

bullets.title: Purpose Limitation
bullets.text: Personal data collected for one specific purpose cannot be used for a different purpose without fresh consent or another legal basis. You cannot collect email addresses for a newsletter and then use them for targeted advertising without separate consent.

bullets.title: Privacy by Design
bullets.text: Privacy protections should be built into systems and processes from the start, not bolted on afterwards. Default settings should offer the highest level of privacy — users should have to opt in to less private options, not opt out.

bullets.title: Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
bullets.text: Software tools that monitor and control data movement — blocking sensitive files from being emailed externally, uploaded to personal cloud storage, or copied to USB drives. DLP is a safety net, not a replacement for good judgment.

bullets.title: Shadow IT
bullets.text: Any application, service, or device used for work purposes without IT's knowledge or approval. Personal Dropbox accounts, consumer messaging apps, and free SaaS tools are common examples. Shadow IT creates data copies outside the organisation's security controls.

bullets.title: Data Sovereignty
bullets.text: The principle that data is subject to the laws of the country where it is stored. Storing EU citizen data on servers outside the EU may violate GDPR. Always check where cloud services store data before using them for personal data.

bullets.title: Records of Processing Activities (ROPA)
bullets.text: A mandatory GDPR requirement for most organisations — a documented record of all personal data processing activities, including what data is held, why, how long it's kept, and who has access. Required for demonstrating compliance.

bullets.title: Pseudonymisation vs Anonymisation
bullets.text: Pseudonymised data has identifying information replaced with a code — it's still personal data because re-identification is possible with the key. Truly anonymised data has had identifying information irreversibly removed and is no longer subject to GDPR. Most 'anonymised' datasets are actually pseudonymised.

quiz.question: An employee's home address and phone number is an example of what kind of data?
options: Public
Internal
Confidential / Personally Identifiable Information (PII)
Trivial
quiz.explanation: PII is any data that can be used to identify a specific individual. It's considered sensitive, confidential data and must be protected to prevent identity theft and privacy violations.

quiz.question: What is the correct way to dispose of a printed document containing confidential client information?
options: Tear it in half and put it in the recycling bin.
Put it in the regular trash can.
Use a cross-cut shredder.
Leave it on your desk for the cleaning crew to handle.
quiz.explanation: Simply throwing away sensitive documents can lead to 'dumpster diving' attacks. A cross-cut shredder makes the documents extremely difficult to reconstruct, which is the standard for secure disposal.

quiz.question: You realize you accidentally clicked a link in a suspicious email. What is the most important first step?
options: Immediately run an antivirus scan.
Turn off your computer and hope for the best.
Don't tell anyone to avoid getting in trouble.
Immediately report the incident to your IT/Security department.
quiz.explanation: Time is critical during a potential breach. Your security team has tools and procedures to investigate and contain threats, but they need to know as soon as possible. Reporting quickly is the most responsible action.

cheatSheet: Classify data based on sensitivity (Public, Internal, Confidential).
Handle confidential data with care and only share on a 'need-to-know' basis.
Securely dispose of sensitive data by shredding physical documents.
Report any suspected security incident to your IT/Security team IMMEDIATELY.

modules.title: Social Engineering & Modern Scams

sections.heading: 1. The Human Element
sections.body: Social engineering is the art of manipulating people so they give up confidential information. Attackers know that humans are often the weakest link in the security chain.

bullets.title: CEO Fraud / Business Email Compromise (BEC)
bullets.text: An attacker impersonates a high-level executive (like the CEO) and emails an employee, usually in finance, urgently requesting a wire transfer or sensitive data.

bullets.title: Deepfakes & AI Voice Cloning
bullets.text: Scammers use AI to clone the voice of a loved one or an executive, calling to demand immediate financial assistance or authorize transactions.

bullets.title: Baiting & Quid Pro Quo
bullets.text: Leaving an infected USB drive in a parking lot (baiting) or offering a fake benefit in exchange for information (quid pro quo).

bullets.title: Pretexting
bullets.text: Creating a fabricated scenario (a pretext) to steal personal information, such as pretending to be a survey researcher or a bank official.

sections.heading: 3. Defending Against Manipulation
sections.body: Always verify unusual requests, especially those involving money or sensitive data, through a secondary channel. If the 'CEO' emails you for an urgent wire transfer, call them or speak to them in person to confirm. Be skeptical of unsolicited offers and never plug in unknown devices.

bullets.title: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT)
bullets.text: Attackers spend significant time researching targets before making contact. They mine LinkedIn profiles, company websites, job postings, social media, press releases, and even GitHub to build a detailed picture of your organisation, roles, tools, and relationships.

bullets.title: Org Chart Harvesting
bullets.text: LinkedIn makes it trivially easy to map who reports to whom, who approves financial transactions, and who has access to sensitive systems. Attackers use this to craft targeted impersonation attacks — knowing exactly who to pretend to be to get what they want.

bullets.title: LinkedIn Oversharing Risks
bullets.text: Posting about internal projects, technologies used, upcoming system migrations, or org changes helps attackers craft convincing pretexts. 'We're migrating to Salesforce next month' gives an attacker the perfect cover for a fake Salesforce support call.

bullets.title: Simulated Phishing Tests
bullets.text: Many organisations run simulated phishing campaigns to test employee awareness. If you click a simulated phishing link, you'll receive training — it's not punitive, it's educational. These tests reveal gaps before real attackers do.

sections.heading: 5. The Six Psychological Principles Attackers Exploit

bullets.title: Authority
bullets.text: People comply with requests from those in power. Attackers impersonate executives, IT teams, auditors, government officials, or law enforcement. Always verify identity through a separate, trusted channel.

bullets.title: Urgency
bullets.text: 'Act now or your account will be closed.' Manufactured time pressure prevents you from thinking critically or following verification procedures. Legitimate requests can always wait for proper verification.

bullets.title: Scarcity
bullets.text: 'Only you can fix this before the deadline.' Creating a sense of unique responsibility makes you feel you can't delegate or double-check.

bullets.title: Social Proof
bullets.text: 'Everyone else in the team has already done this.' If others have complied, it seems safe. Attackers fabricate consensus to lower your guard.

bullets.title: Liking & Reciprocity
bullets.text: Attackers build rapport — complimenting you, finding common ground, or doing a small favour first. Having 'helped' you, they then make their real request. The human impulse to return favours is a powerful lever.

bullets.title: Security Fatigue
bullets.text: Being bombarded with security alerts, 2FA prompts, and access requests eventually makes people approve things without reading them. Attackers exploit this by sending push MFA requests repeatedly until an exhausted user approves.

quiz.question: What is the primary goal of a 'pretexting' attack?
options: To infect your computer with ransomware.
To create a fabricated scenario to trick you into revealing sensitive information.
To guess your password using automated tools.
To physically steal your laptop.
quiz.explanation: Pretexting involves an attacker inventing a scenario (the pretext) to engage a victim and increase the chance they will divulge information or perform actions that would be unlikely in ordinary circumstances.

quiz.question: You find a USB drive in the company parking lot. What is the safest course of action?
options: Plug it into your computer to see who it belongs to so you can return it.
Plug it into a public computer at a library to check its contents safely.
Throw it in the trash.
Give it to your IT or Security department.
quiz.explanation: This is a 'baiting' attack. The USB drive could be loaded with malware that will infect any computer it's plugged into. IT/Security has safe ways to examine such devices.

quiz.question: You receive an urgent email from your company's CEO asking you to immediately wire $50,000 to a new vendor. The email address looks correct. What should you do?
options: Process the wire transfer immediately to avoid upsetting the CEO.
Reply to the email asking for the invoice.
Call the CEO or speak to them in person to verify the request.
Forward the email to the finance department to handle.
quiz.explanation: This is a classic Business Email Compromise (BEC) scenario. Always verify unusual or urgent financial requests through a secondary, trusted communication channel (like a phone call or in-person conversation) before taking action.

cheatSheet: Verify urgent financial requests from executives via a secondary channel (e.g., phone call).
Be aware that AI can now clone voices and create convincing deepfakes.
Never plug unknown USB drives or devices into your computer.
Be skeptical of unsolicited requests for information, even if they seem to come from an authority figure.

modules.title: Financial & Cryptocurrency Scams

sections.heading: 1. The Rise of Crypto Scams
sections.body: With the popularity of cryptocurrency, scammers have developed sophisticated methods to steal digital assets. Because crypto transactions are often irreversible, they are a prime target for fraudsters.

bullets.title: Pig Butchering (Investment Scams)
bullets.text: Scammers build a long-term relationship (often romantic or friendly) online, then convince the victim to invest in a fake crypto platform. They show fake profits to encourage larger investments before disappearing with the money.

bullets.title: Fake Exchanges & Wallets
bullets.text: Malicious apps or websites designed to look like legitimate crypto exchanges. Once you deposit funds or enter your seed phrase, your assets are stolen.

bullets.title: Tech Support & Refund Scams
bullets.text: Scammers claim you are owed a refund or your account is compromised, then guide you to transfer funds or grant remote access to your device.

bullets.title: Giveaway Scams
bullets.text: Fake social media posts from 'celebrities' promising to double any crypto you send to their address.

sections.heading: 3. Protecting Your Assets
sections.body: Never share your wallet's seed phrase or private keys with anyone. Be extremely skeptical of 'guaranteed' high returns or pressure to invest quickly. Only use well-known, established cryptocurrency exchanges and verify URLs carefully before logging in.

bullets.title: Decentralised Finance (DeFi)
bullets.text: DeFi protocols enable financial transactions (lending, trading, earning yield) without a central authority. There is no fraud department, no customer support, and no recourse — if funds are stolen via an exploit or scam, they are gone. The code is the contract.

bullets.title: Smart Contract Vulnerabilities
bullets.text: DeFi runs on smart contracts — self-executing code. Unaudited contracts often contain bugs that attackers can exploit to drain liquidity pools. Only interact with protocols that have been audited by reputable security firms, and even then, risk remains.

bullets.title: Flash Loan Attacks
bullets.text: An attacker borrows a massive amount of cryptocurrency with no collateral (repaid within the same transaction), uses it to manipulate prices or exploit a protocol vulnerability, profits, and repays the loan — all in milliseconds. A single transaction can drain millions.

bullets.title: Yield Farming Scams
bullets.text: Promises of 100%, 500%, or 1000% APY are almost always scams or unsustainable — the 'yield' comes from new investors' money, not genuine returns (a Ponzi structure). When inflows stop, the protocol collapses and early investors withdraw, leaving everyone else with nothing.

bullets.title: Airdrop Scams
bullets.text: You receive free tokens in your wallet. To 'claim' them, you're asked to connect your wallet to a website and approve a transaction. That approval grants the site permission to drain your entire wallet. Never interact with tokens you didn't expect to receive.

bullets.title: NFT Scams & Wash Trading
bullets.text: Fake scarcity is created by artists or promoters buying their own NFTs between wallets to inflate the price history, then selling to real buyers at inflated prices. Research volume, unique buyers, and the creator's history before purchasing.

bullets.title: Address Poisoning
bullets.text: An attacker sends you a tiny transaction from a wallet address that looks almost identical to one you frequently interact with — differing only in a few middle characters. When you copy-paste from your transaction history, you might copy the attacker's address instead. Always verify the full address before sending.

bullets.title: Clipboard Malware
bullets.text: Malware monitors your clipboard and silently replaces any copied cryptocurrency address with the attacker's address. You paste what you think is the right address, verify the first and last few characters, and send funds to the attacker. Verify the full address character by character, or use QR codes from trusted sources.

bullets.title: Wallet Drainers
bullets.text: Malicious smart contracts that, once you approve a transaction, gain permission to withdraw all assets from your wallet. Often hidden in NFT minting sites, fake DeFi protocols, or phishing pages. Treat every wallet connection and approval as a major security decision.

bullets.title: Token Approval Revocation
bullets.text: Every time you interact with a DeFi protocol, you may grant it permission to spend your tokens. These approvals persist indefinitely. Regularly audit and revoke unnecessary token approvals using tools like Revoke.cash to limit your exposure if a protocol is compromised later.

bullets.title: Dusting Attacks
bullets.text: Attackers send tiny amounts of cryptocurrency (dust) to many wallets to trace transaction patterns and de-anonymise wallet owners. Once they know a wallet belongs to a specific individual, they can target them with personalised scams.

bullets.title: QR Code Crypto Scams
bullets.text: Fake 'wallet verification' or 'sync' websites ask you to scan a QR code. The QR code initiates a transaction that sends your funds to the attacker, or connects your wallet for draining. Never scan a QR code to 'verify' or 'sync' a cryptocurrency wallet.

quiz.question: Someone you met online a few months ago suggests you invest in a new crypto trading platform that guarantees a 20% weekly return. They even show you screenshots of their profits. What should you do?
options: Start with a small investment to test the waters.
Ask them for more details about how the platform works.
Recognize this as a likely 'pig butchering' scam and cut off contact.
Borrow money to maximize your potential returns.
quiz.explanation: This is a classic 'pig butchering' investment scam. Scammers build trust over time and use fake platforms showing fabricated profits to lure victims into investing large sums of money.

quiz.question: You receive an email from a popular crypto exchange saying your account needs verification and asks you to enter your 12-word seed phrase. What is the correct action?
options: Enter the seed phrase to ensure your account isn't locked.
Ignore the email, as legitimate exchanges will NEVER ask for your seed phrase.
Reply to the email asking why they need it.
Forward the email to a friend for advice.
quiz.explanation: Your seed phrase is the master key to your crypto wallet. Legitimate exchanges or support staff will never ask for it. Anyone asking for your seed phrase is trying to steal your funds.

quiz.pointsNote: Wireframe shows +250, but module total is advertised as 800 and dashboard math (4350/5100 = 85%) confirms 800. Set to 300 to resolve the inconsistency. Document this decision in the README.
quiz.question: A famous entrepreneur tweets that they are giving back to the community and will double any Bitcoin sent to a specific address. What is this?
options: A legitimate promotional giveaway.
A common giveaway scam, often using compromised verified accounts.
A smart contract testing phase.
An exclusive investment opportunity.
quiz.explanation: Giveaway scams are very common on social media. Scammers often hack verified accounts or create convincing fake profiles to trick people into sending crypto, which is never returned.

cheatSheet: Never share your crypto wallet's seed phrase or private keys with anyone.
Be highly skeptical of online acquaintances pushing 'guaranteed' high-return investments.
Legitimate tech support will never ask you to send cryptocurrency.
If an offer sounds too good to be true (like doubling your money), it is a scam.
